findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a bilateral striated nephrogram, characterised by multiple linear or wedge-shaped regions of alternating hyperattenuation and hypoattenuation radiating through the renal parenchyma. These striations run parallel to the axis of the tubules and collecting ducts and are best appreciated on the excretory phase of a contrast-enhanced CT [1, 2].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include mild diffuse bilateral renal enlargement and focal areas of low attenuation which represent interstitial oedema and microvascular occlusion. There may also be associated perinephric fat stranding or thickening of Gerota's fascia [2].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a focal fluid collection with a thick, irregular wall to suggest a frank renal abscess. Furthermore, there is no evidence of a central obstructing calculus or severe hydronephrosis [2, 3].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of bilateral, wedge-shaped striations of hypoperfusion without gross urinary tract dilatation [2, 4].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Bilateral acute pyelonephritis, given the presence of a bilateral striated nephrogram caused by focal areas of oedema and microvascular occlusion. Although typically unilateral, bilateral involvement can occur when bacterial invasion affects both kidneys [2].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Pyelonephritis, however since bilateral acute pyelonephritis is already established as the primary diagnosis, another major consideration is Renal vein thrombosis (RVT), which typically presents unilaterally and is most often associated with nephrotic syndrome rather than an infectious presentation [4].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Urinary obstruction, given that it can also present with a striated nephrogram, and missing an infected obstructed system is a medical emergency requiring urgent percutaneous nephrostomy. However, obstruction usually results in a unilateral striated nephrogram and typically demonstrates associated hydronephrosis or a visible calculus [4, 5].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate for complications of acute pyelonephritis, such as renal abscess formation or underlying obstruction, which would require urgent intervention [2].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate baseline renal function and anatomy, and to check for any previous uncomplicated urinary tract infections or calculi [2].
management.ctPhases: I would specify a multi-phase CT including a non-contrast phase to detect calculi, a nephrographic phase to optimally demonstrate the striated hypoperfusion, and an excretory phase to assess the collecting system and confirm the orientation of the striations [1, 2].
management.intervention: If there is evidence of an infected obstructed system or a large developing abscess, I would specify an ultrasound-guided percutaneous nephrostomy or drainage to alleviate the sepsis [5].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Urology MDT. My report would clearly document the bilateral nature of the pyelonephritis and explicitly confirm the absence of an obstructing calculus or abscess to guide medical management with appropriate antibiotics [2].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a bilateral striated nephrogram, characterised by multiple linear or wedge-shaped regions of alternating hyperattenuation and hypoattenuation radiating through the renal parenchyma. These striations run parallel to the axis of the tubules and collecting ducts and are best appreciated on the excretory phase of a contrast-enhanced CT [1, 2]. Supporting features include mild diffuse bilateral renal enlargement and focal areas of low attenuation which represent interstitial oedema and microvascular occlusion. There may also be associated perinephric fat stranding or thickening of Gerota's fascia [2]. Importantly, there is no evidence of a focal fluid collection with a thick, irregular wall to suggest a frank renal abscess. Furthermore, there is no evidence of a central obstructing calculus or severe hydronephrosis [2, 3]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of bilateral, wedge-shaped striations of hypoperfusion without gross urinary tract dilatation [2, 4].

The appearances are most in keeping with Bilateral acute pyelonephritis, given the presence of a bilateral striated nephrogram caused by focal areas of oedema and microvascular occlusion. Although typically unilateral, bilateral involvement can occur when bacterial invasion affects both kidneys [2]. The principle differential would be Pyelonephritis, however since bilateral acute pyelonephritis is already established as the primary diagnosis, another major consideration is Renal vein thrombosis (RVT), which typically presents unilaterally and is most often associated with nephrotic syndrome rather than an infectious presentation [4]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Urinary obstruction, given that it can also present with a striated nephrogram, and missing an infected obstructed system is a medical emergency requiring urgent percutaneous nephrostomy. However, obstruction usually results in a unilateral striated nephrogram and typically demonstrates associated hydronephrosis or a visible calculus [4, 5].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to evaluate for complications of acute pyelonephritis, such as renal abscess formation or underlying obstruction, which would require urgent intervention [2]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate baseline renal function and anatomy, and to check for any previous uncomplicated urinary tract infections or calculi [2]. I would specify a multi-phase CT including a non-contrast phase to detect calculi, a nephrographic phase to optimally demonstrate the striated hypoperfusion, and an excretory phase to assess the collecting system and confirm the orientation of the striations [1, 2]. If there is evidence of an infected obstructed system or a large developing abscess, I would specify an ultrasound-guided percutaneous nephrostomy or drainage to alleviate the sepsis [5]. This should go to the Urology MDT. My report would clearly document the bilateral nature of the pyelonephritis and explicitly confirm the absence of an obstructing calculus or abscess to guide medical management with appropriate antibiotics [2].
